narrative:
Chris Smith has been in Congress since 1981, which means he has now served under eight presidents, survived twenty-two elections, and outlasted the Soviet Union. He is tied with Hal Rogers as the longest-currently-serving member of the House and holds the record for most laws authored among all 435 representatives [1]. At some point, a career this long stops being a resume and becomes a geological feature.

The strange thing about Smith is that he genuinely defies easy categorization. He is one of the most vocally anti-abortion members of Congress, co-chairing the Congressional Pro-Life Caucus for over 30 years and fighting to defund Planned Parenthood at every turn [2]. But he also co-sponsored mandatory background checks for gun sales (HR 8), backed a bump stock ban after the Las Vegas shooting, opposes concealed carry, and once received a 100% rating from the Brady Campaign to Prevent Gun Violence [3]. Try finding another Republican who can say that.

His signature legislative achievement is the Trafficking Victims Protection Act of 2000, which created the first comprehensive federal framework to combat human trafficking. That law has led to more than 4,800 convictions, including those of Jeffrey Epstein and Ghislaine Maxwell [2]. He has authored five anti-trafficking laws total. He also wrote three major autism research bills, most recently the Autism CARES Act of 2024, which provides $1.95 billion over five years [4]. These are not performative gestures -- they are serious, bipartisan laws that have changed lives.

Then there is the other Chris Smith. He has raised concerns about offshore wind turbines interfering with military aviation and pushed amendments requiring presidential certification that turbines are safe [5]. He chairs the Congressional-Executive Commission on China and the Tom Lantos Human Rights Commission [1], where his hawkish foreign policy instincts align more comfortably with the party mainstream.

Smith won reelection in 2024 with 68% of the vote [6] and is running again in 2026. His district loves him, or at least cannot imagine anyone else. After 45 years, the man and the seat have become indistinguishable -- which is either a testament to constituent service or the strongest possible argument for term limits.

Victoria Spartz is one of the more unusual figures in Congress, a Ukrainian-born Republican representing Indiana's 5th District — the affluent suburbs north of Indianapolis — who has built a brand around being an unpredictable fiscal hawk with a personal story that practically writes itself. Born in Nosivka, Soviet Ukraine, Spartz emigrated to the United States in 2000, earned a master's in professional accountancy from Indiana University, and worked as a CPA and real estate investor before entering the Indiana State Senate in 2017 [1]. She won her House seat in 2020 after a bruising primary and has since become known for bucking party leadership, making fiery floor speeches about fiscal responsibility, and generating a steady stream of headlines about her own internal contradictions. She briefly announced her retirement in 2023, then reversed course and ran again in 2024, winning her seat back after a competitive primary [2].

Spartz's relationship with the 80% Bill agenda is genuinely complicated, which is more than can be said for most of her colleagues. She has been one of the louder Republican voices calling for a full audit of the Pentagon, citing her accounting background and her stated belief that the Department of Defense's inability to pass an audit is a national scandal [3]. She has also supported congressional term limits and voted to release the Epstein files [4]. On protecting U.S. farmland from foreign purchases, she has been vocal — a position with particular resonance given her Ukrainian background and awareness of how foreign land acquisition can become a geopolitical weapon [5]. These are real positions that align with strong public majorities.

But the alignment ends where the donor dollars begin. Spartz has consistently opposed Medicare drug price negotiation, voting against the Inflation Reduction Act and its drug-pricing provisions [6]. She has shown no interest in banning congressional stock trading, despite her background in finance and accounting — precisely the expertise that would make her a credible champion for the issue. On campaign finance reform, banning corporate PACs, and the DISCLOSE Act, she is absent. She has not supported banning dark money or overturning Citizens United, and her record on fair elections and gerrymandering reform is nonexistent.

The money tells a familiar story. In her 2024 cycle, Spartz raised significant sums from the real estate and finance sectors — industries in which she has personal financial interests [7]. Health sector donors, including pharmaceutical and insurance interests, have been consistent contributors, which provides useful context for her opposition to drug price negotiation [8]. She has also benefited from leadership PACs and traditional Republican fundraising networks. Her personal financial disclosures reveal extensive real estate holdings in Indiana, making her silence on banning corporate purchases of single-family homes a matter of direct self-interest rather than mere ideological alignment [9].

Spartz is the rare member of Congress who seems genuinely uncomfortable with the institution she serves in — she has publicly clashed with House GOP leadership, been one of the holdout votes that paralyzed Speaker elections, and frequently votes against her own party's bills on fiscal grounds [10]. That instinct for independence is real, but it is also selective. She will buck leadership on procedural votes and spending bills, but she will not cross the donor class on stock trading, dark money, or corporate PAC bans. Victoria Spartz came to America from a country where oligarchs captured the government. She should need no reminder of what it looks like when wealthy interests write the rules — and yet, on the reforms that would prevent exactly that outcome here, she is largely missing in action.

Janice Schakowsky has represented Illinois's 9th Congressional District — the affluent, liberal stretch of Chicago's North Shore and northern suburbs — since 1999, making her one of the longest-serving progressive women in the House [1]. Before Congress, she was a consumer advocate who helped pass Illinois's expiration date labeling law, and that origin story has echoed through a career built around consumer protection, health care, and campaign finance reform [2]. At 81, she is a senior member of the Energy and Commerce Committee and sits on the Budget Committee, positions that place her at the intersection of several 80% Bill issues [3].

On the 80% Bill ledger, Schakowsky's record is extensive and largely aligned. She has been a career-long champion of Medicare drug price negotiation, having introduced or cosponsored versions of the Lower Drug Costs Now Act across multiple sessions of Congress [4]. She cosponsored the DISCLOSE Act and has been one of the most consistent voices in the House for overturning Citizens United, backing the Democracy for All constitutional amendment [5]. She supports banning congressional stock trading, having cosponsored the TRUST in Congress Act [6], and has been a reliable cosponsor of paid family leave legislation through the FAMILY Act [7]. On war powers, she voted to repeal the 2001 and 2002 AUMFs [8]. She has supported Social Security expansion through the Social Security 2100 Act [9] and has been a cosponsor of the For the People Act, which addresses fair elections and gerrymandering [10].

Where Schakowsky's record gets interesting is on the issues where her committee perch creates tension. As a senior member of Energy and Commerce, she has jurisdiction over right-to-repair issues and has been a supporter of consumer-side repair legislation [11]. She has backed junk fee legislation and sponsored consumer protection bills targeting hidden charges in health care and financial services [12]. On taxing the ultra-wealthy, she introduced the Tax Excessive CEO Pay Act, which would raise corporate tax rates on companies where CEO-to-worker pay ratios exceed 50-to-1 [13]. She has not, however, been a prominent voice on Pentagon audits or protecting U.S. farmland from foreign acquisition — issues that do not neatly fit her committee portfolio.

The financial picture is a study in progressive fundraising. Schakowsky's top contributors over her career have been labor unions — AFSCME, the American Federation of Teachers, and SEIU feature prominently — along with ideological groups like Emily's List and J Street [14]. She has pledged to refuse corporate PAC money in recent cycles [15]. Her individual donor base skews toward the professional class of her North Shore district: lawyers, educators, and retirees. For a 26-year incumbent, her fundraising totals are remarkably modest, suggesting either admirable restraint or the comfortable arithmetic of a district where she has never faced a competitive general election [16].

Schakowsky is, by nearly any measure, one of the most naturally aligned members of Congress with the 80% Bill. Her record on drug pricing, dark money, stock trading, paid leave, war powers, Social Security, fair elections, junk fees, and CEO pay is long and documented. The gaps — farmland protection, Pentagon audits, term limits — are areas of silence rather than opposition, though one might note that a representative entering her fourteenth term is an unlikely evangelist for the term limits cause [1]. She is proof that longevity and principle are not mutually exclusive, even if her continued presence in Congress is itself an argument for one of the bills she declines to support.

Michael Keith Simpson has represented Idaho's 2nd Congressional District since 1999, making him one of the longest-serving House members from the Gem State. A dentist by training — he ran Simpson Family Dental Practice in Blackfoot for over two decades before entering Congress — Simpson serves on the powerful House Appropriations Committee, where he chairs the Subcommittee on Interior, Environment, and Related Agencies and previously chaired Energy and Water Development [1]. He is widely considered a moderate Republican, a designation earned by supporting DACA, backing work visas for undocumented farmworkers, and most memorably by voting against Jim Jordan's Speaker nomination all three times in October 2023 [2]. In deep-red Idaho, that moderation has occasionally drawn fire: in 2014, the Club for Growth bankrolled Tea Party challenger Bryan Smith against him, triggering the most expensive congressional primary in the country, with outside groups pouring roughly $3 million into the race. Simpson won handily, 62 percent to 38 percent [3].

Despite his moderate reputation, Simpson's alignment with the 80% Bill is thin. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act in 2022, which included Medicare drug price negotiation — the bill passed the House on a strict party-line vote with every Republican opposed [4]. He has not cosponsored legislation to ban congressional stock trading, impose term limits, create a lifetime lobbying ban, or ban corporate PACs. On the DISCLOSE Act and campaign finance transparency, his record is similarly blank. He has not championed police body camera mandates, paid family leave, right to repair, or junk fee bans. His signature legislative passion is Idaho-specific: a sweeping salmon restoration plan that would remove four federal dams on the lower Snake River, a position that puts him at odds with many in his own party [5]. He has also been a tireless advocate for Idaho National Laboratory funding, securing billions in appropriations for nuclear energy research over his career [6].

Simpson's relationship with earmarks illustrates a particular brand of Washington pragmatism. He has defended the earmarks process as essential for directing federal dollars to Idaho, breaking with Republican orthodoxy on the subject [7]. Yet in 2022, he submitted 15 funding requests for Idaho community projects in an appropriations bill and then voted against the very bill that contained them [8]. It is the legislative equivalent of ordering dinner and then complaining about the restaurant.

His donor profile reflects his Appropriations Committee perch. The American Dental Association PAC, the American Hospital Association, and the National Association of Realtors have been among his most generous supporters, with the dental and hospital groups spending more than $400,000 combined on his behalf in 2014 alone [9]. A union-backed PAC dropped $200,000 to support him during that same primary fight — unusual for a Republican, but a testament to how much organized labor preferred the moderate Simpson to his Tea Party challenger [10]. His top contributing industries over his career include health professionals, electric utilities, and real estate, all sectors with significant business before the Appropriations Committee [11].

Simpson is the kind of Republican who can credibly claim independence — he bucks his party on immigration, on earmarks, on the occasional Speaker vote. But on the twenty planks of the 80% Bill, that independence never seems to arrive. He has not used his considerable appropriations leverage to advance any of the popular reforms the bill represents, preferring instead the quiet, transactional work of steering federal dollars to Idaho while leaving the systemic questions untouched.

Adam Schiff served 23 years in the House representing parts of Los Angeles before winning California's 2024 Senate race to fill the seat left by the late Dianne Feinstein, spending roughly $38 million to get there [1][2]. A former federal prosecutor, Schiff became a household name as chair of the House Intelligence Committee during Trump's first impeachment, earning both a national following among Democrats and a congressional censure from Republicans [3]. He is now the junior senator from California, a member of one of the most visible delegations in Washington, and a man who has built a career on the idea that institutions matter -- which makes his relationship with the 80% Bill worth examining closely.

On the substance, Schiff's alignment with the twenty bills is genuinely strong -- stronger than most of his colleagues, and not just on the easy ones. He introduced the TRUST in Congress Act, a bipartisan bill to ban members and their families from trading individual stocks, making him one of the earlier and more credible champions of that cause [4]. He has introduced a constitutional amendment to overturn Citizens United every year since 2013, pushing to outlaw dark money and allow reasonable restrictions on political spending [5]. He co-sponsored the DISCLOSE Act and championed the For the People Act, which included provisions to end partisan gerrymandering and create public campaign financing [6]. He supported the Inflation Reduction Act with its Medicare drug price negotiation provisions, and has called for expanding the program to cover all drugs [7]. He has pushed to repeal outdated AUMFs, proposing an 18-month sunset for the 2001 authorization while consolidating use-of-force authorities under proper congressional oversight [8]. He has fought to protect Social Security from cuts and privatization, and supported paid family leave in the Build Back Better framework [9][10]. And on the Epstein files, Schiff and Senator Durbin demanded an expedited independent audit of the DOJ's handling of the records, raising concerns about possible tampering [11]. That is a long list of genuine engagement with supermajority priorities.

Where it gets complicated is the space between Schiff's reform rhetoric and his fundraising reality. He pledged to reject corporate PAC money during his Senate campaign, which sounds admirable until you learn what that pledge actually covers. As CalMatters documented, Schiff continued accepting money from trade association PACs, professional organization PACs, and individual corporate executives -- categories that accounted for roughly $2.9 million in corporate PAC contributions and at least $648,000 from self-identified CEOs and executives over his career [12]. The National Association of Realtors, the largest trade group representing the real estate industry, was among his top non-corporate PAC donors [12]. His 2024 Senate campaign was a fundraising juggernaut: he entered the race with $35 million in cash on hand, more than any presidential candidate at the time, and ultimately spent over $85 million on the contest [2][13]. You do not raise that kind of money by being a threat to the donor class. You raise it by being a reliable investment.

Follow the money and the pattern sharpens. Schiff's top contributing sectors over his career have included lawyers and law firms, the entertainment industry, and real estate -- all industries with significant stakes in tax policy, intellectual property law, and the very financial regulations the 80% Bill seeks to reform [14]. He received contributions from lobbyists even as he railed against their influence [15]. He supported Pentagon budget cuts in principle, backing amendments to reduce the defense budget by 10%, but never made military spending reform a signature cause the way he made impeachment or democracy reform into personal brands [7]. He talks about auditing the Pentagon the way a dentist talks about your next cleaning -- important, certainly, but always somehow scheduled for later.

The bottom line on Schiff is that he is one of the better-aligned members of the Senate on the 80% Bill's twenty items, and that alignment appears largely sincere rather than performative. He put his name on the stock trading ban before it was fashionable. He has been relentless on Citizens United. He pushed on the Epstein files when it was politically awkward. But he also built a $38 million Senate campaign on the very fundraising infrastructure that several of these bills aim to dismantle, and his pledge to reject corporate PAC money had enough loopholes to park a lobbying firm through. Schiff is the kind of reformer who genuinely wants to change the system -- just not so fast that it inconveniences his next campaign.

Linda T. Sanchez has represented California's 38th district since 2013, and before that the 39th since her first election in 2002. A former labor lawyer who cut her teeth at IBEW Local 441 and served as executive director of the Orange County Central Labor Council, she and her sister Loretta became the first pair of sisters to serve in Congress simultaneously [1]. She sits on the powerful Ways and Means Committee, where tax policy is written, and the Ethics Committee, where it is theoretically enforced [2]. Her working-class bona fides are real -- she is the daughter of Mexican immigrant parents and worked her way through UC Berkeley and UCLA Law [3]. All of which makes the gaps between her biography and her record worth examining.

On paper, Sanchez aligns with a healthy chunk of the 80% Bill. She voted for the Inflation Reduction Act, which finally empowered Medicare to negotiate prescription drug prices and capped insulin at $35 a month [4]. She has championed paid family leave, voting for the Build Back Better Act's guarantee of four weeks for all Americans and repeatedly calling for a national program [5][6]. She co-sponsored the For the People Act (H.R. 1), the sweeping democracy reform package that would end partisan gerrymandering, require dark money disclosure, and push back on Citizens United [7]. She has voiced support for expanding Social Security benefits and switching to CPI-E so they keep pace with actual senior costs [8]. And she co-sponsored the HOPE for Homeownership Act to rein in corporate landlords buying up single-family homes [9].

But the contradictions are where it gets interesting. Sanchez has been a vocal advocate for banning congressional stock trading -- and then violated the very STOCK Act she championed, filing a late disclosure on a sale of up to $15,000 in Cisco Systems stock, a company that holds numerous federal government contracts [10]. Her office blamed the stock on a gift to her infant son at his baptism, which is the kind of excuse that sounds more creative the longer you think about it. The Ethics Committee waived her $200 fine because she squeaked inside a monthlong "grace period," a loophole that exists precisely for lawmakers who write the rules [10]. Meanwhile, she sits on the Ethics Committee itself, tasked with policing the very behavior she engaged in. And then there is the matter of her husband, James Sullivan, who was indicted in 2018 for theft of federal funds -- using an $800,000 "secret slush fund" at a Connecticut municipal energy cooperative to bankroll trips to the Kentucky Derby and a luxury golf resort [11][12]. Sullivan was sentenced to six months in federal prison [13]. Sanchez withdrew her bid for House Democratic Caucus chair the day the indictment dropped, citing "an unexpected family matter" [14].

Sanchez's campaign coffers tell a familiar Ways and Means story. Over her career, top contributing sectors have included labor unions, lawyers and law firms, and real estate interests -- all of which have significant business before her committee [15]. Her leadership PAC, "Stand with Sanchez," has distributed funds across the Democratic ecosystem [16]. None of this is unusual for a senior Ways and Means member, which is precisely the point: the system works as designed, and the design is the problem. She represents a safe blue district, raises money from the industries she oversees, and votes the right way on most populist issues while the structural corruption she claims to oppose keeps humming along around her. The stock-ban advocate who violated the stock act, sitting on the committee that enforces ethics, married to a man who went to prison for raiding public funds -- at some point the irony becomes load-bearing.

David Scott has represented Georgia's 13th Congressional District since 2003, making him one of the longest-serving members of the Georgia delegation. A Wharton MBA holder and former small-business owner, Scott sits on the House Financial Services Committee, where he chairs subcommittees overseeing capital markets, securities, and consumer credit [1]. In 2021, he became the first Black chairman of the House Agriculture Committee, a historic achievement that would later become a source of intra-party friction [2]. Now in his eighties and seeking a thirteenth term, Scott has watched as nine primary challengers line up for 2026, many citing concerns that transcend ideology and land squarely on the question of capacity [3].

On the issues that compose the 80% Bill, Scott's record is a study in selective engagement. He voted for the STOCK Act in 2012, the original (and widely regarded as toothless) attempt to curb congressional insider trading [4]. But he has not cosponsored any of the more muscular stock-trading bans introduced since, nor has he championed term limits, lobbying restrictions, or campaign finance overhauls like the DISCLOSE Act. On Medicare drug negotiation, he voted for the Inflation Reduction Act when it passed the House. His Financial Services Committee perch puts him at the center of debates over junk fees, consumer protection, and Wall Street oversight, yet his legislative footprint on those fronts has been notably light for someone with two decades of seniority.

The most revealing episode in Scott's career may be the 2016 incident in which he read, nearly verbatim, from testimony written by Richard Hunt, the top lobbyist for the Consumer Bankers Association, during a House hearing on payday lending. Scott even inadvertently used the lobbyist's first-person pronoun, referring to "positive feedback from our borrowers" [5]. OpenSecrets reported that payday lenders had contributed more than $72,000 to Scott's campaigns at the time [6]. His top donor industries over his career track neatly with his committee assignments: the finance, insurance, and real estate sector has been a consistent and generous patron, as has the securities and investment industry [7]. For a member who oversees the very industries that fund his campaigns, the arrangement is remarkably tidy.

In December 2024, House Democrats quietly removed Scott from his ranking position on the Agriculture Committee after colleagues expressed concerns about his ability to communicate strategy and manage the panel's work [8]. He had missed two weeks of votes due to back issues and was increasingly seen arriving to the House floor in a wheelchair [9]. Public records later revealed he had not voted in six consecutive elections, including the 2024 presidential race [10]. The portrait that emerges is of a congressman who has held powerful committee seats for two decades, drawn substantial financial support from the industries he regulates, and now faces serious questions about whether he is still doing the job at all.

Marilyn Strickland's career arc is a masterclass in institutional shape-shifting. She went from Starbucks corporate to two terms as mayor of Tacoma to president and CEO of the Seattle Metropolitan Chamber of Commerce to Congress, a trajectory so seamlessly corporate-to-political that it should come with a LinkedIn endorsement from the revolving door itself. One of the first three Korean-American women elected to Congress, Strickland made history in 2020 -- and then proceeded to vote with President Biden's stated position 100% of the time, which is less "independent voice" and more "reliable echo."

Before arriving in Washington, D.C., Strickland ran the Seattle Metro Chamber of Commerce, a 137-year-old nonprofit representing business interests. In that role, she led the opposition to Seattle's head tax -- a levy on large employers like Amazon designed to fund affordable housing and homeless services. Progressives watched Strickland work "side-by-side with Amazon in a blatant attempt to buy the Seattle City Council," as one critic put it, killing the tax that would have asked the richest corporations to pay their fair share. As mayor of Tacoma, she opposed the $15 minimum wage campaign and fought against paid sick days legislation. Her opponent in the 2020 primary, progressive Beth Doglio, argued that Strickland's record showed "consistent fealty to corporate interests." Labor unions agreed, backing Doglio instead.

Strickland disputes this characterization, calling it "a false narrative" and preferring the language of pragmatism over ideology. But pragmatism in Congress has a funny way of looking exactly like the status quo. She earned a 6% score from Heritage Action (meaning she votes progressive by their standards) and a strong AFL-CIO scorecard once in office, but the question isn't how she votes on party-line bills -- it's whether she'll champion the structural reforms that challenge the corporate power she spent a career serving.

On the 80% Bill's twenty policies, Strickland is a study in polite avoidance. Ban corporate PACs? She came to Congress backed by corporate money. Tax the ultra-wealthy? She ran a chamber of commerce that exists to prevent exactly that. Ban corporate home buying and protect farmland? Her district includes Joint Base Lewis-McChord and the port city of Olympia, where real estate developers and military contractors hold real sway. She'll support popular Democratic priorities like paid family leave and Medicare drug negotiation when leadership tells her to, but the items that would restructure power -- the lobbying ban, the DISCLOSE Act, Pentagon audits, term limits -- remain conspicuously absent from her priority list. Strickland isn't a villain; she's a product. And the factory that built her is still taking orders.

Adrian Smith has represented Nebraska's 3rd Congressional District since 2007, a vast rural swath of cattle ranches and cornfields where Republican primaries are the only elections that matter. A senior member of the House Ways and Means Committee, Smith has built a nearly two-decade career as a reliable vote for tax cuts, deregulation, and whatever the agriculture lobby asks for [1][2]. He has won ten consecutive terms, which tells you either that he is exceptionally good at his job or that NE-3 voters would re-elect a fence post if it had an R next to its name.

On the 80% Bill scorecard, Smith is a near-perfect zero. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation, calling it a "government takeover" of drug pricing [3][4]. He voted against H.R. 1, the For the People Act, which contained anti-gerrymandering provisions, the DISCLOSE Act to ban dark money, and campaign finance reform -- knocking out at least three items on the 80% Bill list with one vote [5]. He voted against the Social Security Fairness Act, siding with the 75 House members who opposed expanding benefits even as the program faces a funding shortfall [6]. And he helped write the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, which slashed the corporate tax rate from 35% to 21% and delivered the lion's share of its benefits to top earners -- the opposite of closing tax loopholes or taxing the ultra-wealthy [7][8].

Where Smith does show a pulse is on farmland protection -- he supports legislation to prevent foreign adversaries from purchasing American agricultural land, a position that aligns with his district's interests [9]. He also endorsed Senator Fischer's paid family leave tax incentive, though that employer-voluntary approach is a far cry from the universal paid family leave most Americans support [10]. On term limits, the congressman who has served nearly twenty years in office has offered no enthusiasm for limiting his own tenure. On banning congressional stock trading, Smith's record is particularly rich: he violated the STOCK Act by filing late disclosures for his wife's stock purchases, paid the $200 fine, and moved on [11]. Apparently the man who cannot follow existing stock trading rules sees no need for stricter ones.

Follow the money and the pattern clarifies. Smith raised over $1 million in the 2024 cycle alone, with heavy PAC support from agribusiness, insurance, and financial services industries -- the same sectors regulated by his Ways and Means perch [12][13]. The U.S. Chamber of Commerce has hosted him for tax reform roundtables, and the Nebraska Farm Bureau PAC has endorsed him repeatedly [14][15]. The AFL-CIO gives him a 10% lifetime score, meaning he votes with working people about as often as Nebraska gets a hurricane [2].

The bottom line on Adrian Smith is straightforward: he represents the interests that fund him with mechanical consistency. He opposes Medicare drug negotiation, election reform, dark money disclosure, Social Security expansion, and stock trading bans while championing tax cuts for corporations and collecting PAC checks from the industries he oversees. On the 80% Bill, Smith is proof that in a safe enough district, a congressman can oppose nearly everything most Americans want and never once worry about the consequences.

Steve Scalise is the Republican representative for Louisiana's 1st Congressional District, serving since 2008, and has held the title of House Majority Leader since 2023 -- making him the second most powerful Republican in the chamber. A former state legislator who survived both a near-fatal shooting at a 2017 congressional baseball practice and a blood cancer diagnosis in 2023, Scalise is a man who has genuinely stared down death twice and returned to the job of ensuring that popular legislation never reaches the House floor. He sits on the Energy and Commerce Committee and has been one of the most prolific fundraisers in Republican politics, steering tens of millions through his leadership PAC and joint fundraising committees [1].

On the 80% bills, Scalise's record is a masterclass in opposition to things most Americans want. He voted against the Inflation Reduction Act, which included Medicare drug price negotiation -- a provision supported by roughly 80% of voters -- and called Democrats' approach "socialist, government-dictated" pricing [2]. As Majority Leader, he has blocked or declined to schedule votes on the DISCLOSE Act, corporate PAC bans, dark money restrictions, and lobbying reform. When HR 1, the sweeping democracy reform bill, came up, Scalise framed campaign finance transparency as "a choice between freedom and socialism" [3]. He voted to overturn the 2020 election results by objecting to Arizona's and Pennsylvania's electoral votes in the hours after the January 6 insurrection [4], a position that makes his rhetorical commitment to "fair elections" somewhat difficult to take seriously.

On the congressional stock trading ban, Scalise has played an interesting double game. As Majority Leader, he publicly supported moving a GOP-backed stock trading bill through committee and said he wanted a full House vote [5]. But the bill that advanced was the weaker Republican version -- one that stops short of a full trading ban -- and critics argue leadership slow-walked the process for years before finally allowing a committee vote. On term limits, paid family leave, taxing the ultra-wealthy, auditing the Pentagon, and protecting farmland from corporate buyers, Scalise has been either silent or actively opposed.

Follow the money and Scalise's priorities come into sharp focus. His Team Scalise joint fundraising committee raised over $30 million in the 2024 cycle, and his Eye of the Tiger leadership PAC distributed more money to federal candidates than any other leadership PAC in the 118th Congress [1]. The oil and gas industry has been particularly generous: Koch Industries and Valero Energy rank among his top PAC donors, and he collected $368,291 from oil and gas interests in the 2023-2024 cycle alone -- enough to make him the fourth-largest House recipient of industry cash [6]. After January 6, six major fossil fuel companies sent him another $52,500 [7]. For a man who sits on the Energy and Commerce Committee, this is not a conflict of interest so much as it is the entire interest.

The bottom line on Steve Scalise is that he is the man most responsible for deciding which bills reach the House floor, and not a single one of the twenty 80% bills has made it through on his watch. He has the power to let Americans vote on drug pricing, stock trading bans, dark money disclosure, and term limits -- and he has used that power to ensure they do not. The oil and gas money flows, the leadership PAC distributes, and the popular will waits in a filing cabinet somewhere in the Majority Leader's office.
